coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of metal framework-modified carbon dots with super large stokes shift using Hami melon as a green precursor for detecting thiophanate-methyl residue in leafy vegetables

coredata.dc:description: Consuming leafy vegetables with excessive thiophanate-methyl (TM) residue poses serious risks to human health. To realize rapidly and sensitively detecting TM in leafy vegetables, we developed a fluorescent probe based on zeolitic imidazolate framework-8-modified carbon dots using Hami melon as the green precursor (HM-CDs@ZIF-8). Meanwhile, the mechanism of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 for detecting TM was investigated and explained. The results of the performance tests showed that the prepared HM-CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, robust anti-interference capability, reliable reproducibility and repeatability, and long-term stability. After optimization experiments, the fluorescence intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 showed a strong linear correlation with the concentration of TM (0.00171–3.4239 mol/L) with a detection limit of 2.025 μmol/L. The HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was successfully applied to determine TM in spiked leafy vegetables with satisfactory recoveries of 96–105%. The relative standard deviations were in the range of 0.26–2.55%. The sensor has a promising application for detecting TM in leafy vegetables.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Consuming leafy vegetables with excessive thiophanate-methyl (TM) residue poses serious risks to human health. To realize rapidly and sensitively detecting TM in leafy vegetables, we developed a fluorescent probe based on zeolitic imidazolate framework-8-modified carbon dots using Hami melon as the green precursor (HM-CDs@ZIF-8). Meanwhile, the mechanism of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 for detecting TM was investigated and explained. The results of the performance tests showed that the prepared HM-CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, robust anti-interference capability, reliable reproducibility and repeatability, and long-term stability. After optimization experiments, the fluorescence intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 showed a strong linear correlation with the concentration of TM (0.00171–3.4239 mol/L) with a detection limit of 2.025 μmol/L. The HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was successfully applied to determine TM in spiked leafy vegetables with satisfactory recoveries of 96–105%. The relative standard deviations were in the range of 0.26–2.55%. The sensor has a promising application for detecting TM in leafy vegetables.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In modern agriculture, pesticides are widely used to control crop pests and weeds but can lead to the inevitable problem of pesticide residues after the crop harvest. Consumption of leafy vegetables with excessive pesticide residues poses certain risks to human health (Phopin et al., 2022). Thiophanate-methyl (TM) is a common broad-spectrum fungicide commonly used for the control of botrytis cinerea (gray mold) (Wikipedia, 2024), colletotrichum spp. (anthracnose) (Kamle & Kumar, 2016), and sclerotinia sclerotiorum (sclerotinia disease) (Crouch et al., 2014) in leafy vegetables, and its mechanism of action is to inhibit cell division to achieve sterilization. TM is teratogenic and reproductively toxic, and prolonged exposure to TM poses a high risk to human health (Han et al., 2019). Meanwhile, TM degrades slowly in nature and is highly susceptible to contamination of water sources in the natural environment (Flores et al., 2020). Therefore, developing a susceptible detection method for TM residue in leafy vegetables is significant. A variety of techniques for the detection and identification of pesticide residues have been applied in the field of food safety, including high-performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet (HPLC-UV) (Kardani et al., 2023), liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Zhu et al., 2022), electrochemical assay (EC) (Li et al., 2022), gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) (Zhou et al., 2021) and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Felipe et al., 2024). Although these methods are sensitive and accurate in detecting TM residue, they necessitate complex pre-processing and expensive equipment before analysis, making the detection steps cumbersome and time-consuming. The above drawbacks limit the application scenarios of these detection methods (Wang et al., 2020). Therefore, it is crucial to develop a simple, time-saving, efficient, and sensitive method to detect TM residue in leafy vegetables by overcoming the shortcomings of the above detection methods.

Fluorescence (FL) analysis based on carbon dots (CDs) has gained wide attention (Akbari et al., 2024; Sasikumar et al., 2024; Zhang, Xu, et al., 2022) in biosensing and imaging and food safety detection due to its advantages of low toxicity, good conductivity (Chaudhary et al., 2024), high stability, low cost (Parashar Kumar et al., 2023), fast response, and simplicity (Li et al., 2020). Using hyaluronic acid and citric acid, Wen et al. (2020) achieved vivid blue CDs for the sensitive detection of ferric ions (Fe3+) and folic acid (FA) through a one-step hydrothermal approach. Their findings indicated that the FL quenching ratio of the synthesized CDs exhibited robust linear correlations with Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 900.0 μM and FA concentrations ranging from 4.0 to 100.0 μM. Moreover, they attained low limits of detections (LODs) for Fe3+ (0.008 μM) and FA (0.49 μM). Xu et al. (2019) devised a straightforward and effective FL analysis technique for L-Cysteine (L-Cys). The sensor demonstrated a wide linear detection range (0–100 μM) and relatively low LOD (0.35 μM) for L-Cys. This uncomplicated method exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting L-Cys. Wu et al. (2022) developed an ultra-sensitive nanosensor for glyphosate based on CDs. This nanosensor enables trace detection of glyphosate within a wide detection range of 50–1300 ng/mL, with spiked recoveries ranging from 93.3% to 110.0%. Moreover, the nanosensor has been utilized as a dual-mode visual sensor and a smart sensing membrane capable of identifying glyphosate on vegetable surfaces, thereby demonstrating promising practical applicability. The findings from these studies highlight the outstanding detection performance and potential practical applications of CDs. Different synthesis methods lead to different FL properties for developing highly efficient CDs. The synthesis of CDs consists mainly of top-down and bottom-up routes using chemical, electrochemical, and physical techniques (Ma, Sun, et al., 2020). The top-down route usually involves decomposing larger carbon structures (e.g., carbon nanotubes, graphite, and activated carbon) into CDs by arc discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical oxidation. The bottom-up route involves the synthesis of carbon dots from small precursors (e.g., organic sugars, organic acids, and phenolic compounds) via hydrothermal/solvent heat treatment, loaded synthesis, and microwave synthesis. Notably, recent studies have looked at developing innovative and robust synthetic methods to fabricate FL CDs, e.g., from bagasse (Shehla et al., 2023), frangipani (He et al., 2023), and spinach (Wang, Zhao, et al., 2023), among others. Despite many advances in this field, there is still a need to explore innovative and cost-effective methods for CDs synthesis. The hydrothermal method, among the aforementioned approaches, is characterized by its simplicity, low cost, environmental friendliness (Liu et al., 2019; Wang, Liu, et al., 2022), mild reaction conditions, and strong controllability. Therefore, it is widely employed in the synthesis research of CDs. Additionally, the selection of precursors is crucial. Compared to non-renewable and potentially hazardous chemical materials, choosing biomass materials as precursors for synthesizing CDs aligns more with the concept of sustainable development. Furthermore, it offers the advantages of safety and low cost. Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) is a nutrient-rich fruit whose primary nutrients include vitamins, sugars, amino acids, carotenoids, and fiber. These components can be used as carbon elements required for CDs, and our study chose Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) as the green precursor for CDs synthesis. Stokes shift is the redshifting of the emission spectrum relative to the absorption spectrum in FL spectroscopy. The performance of CDs analysis with larger Stokes shift is superior (Hua et al., 2023), as larger Stokes shift prevents the influence of excitation light and the luminescence of the biomass itself on the detection results (Sun et al., 2024). In addition, nitrogen doping can change the electronic structure of CDs to enhance their FL performance and chemical stability (Hua et al., 2020). Compared with conventional fluorescent probes, FL-enhanced probes can have a lower LOD and effectively detect samples with low analyte concentrations. CDs must be stored at low temperatures for long periods after synthesis, inevitably leading to their aggregation at low temperatures and affecting their analytical performance. Therefore, measures need to be taken to solve this problem. Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a form of metal-organic framework (MOF) that possesses a zeolite-like structure with an imidazolium salt skeleton. It is known for its strong chemical stability and remarkable capacity to absorb light. ZIF-8 has a wide internal surface area and a homogeneous cavity, allowing it to be equally dispersed in the cavity (Liu et al., 2020) and preventing aggregation, resulting in more excellent long-term stability.

Based on the above considerations, in this study, we synthesized a fluorescent probe based on ZIF-8-modified carbon dots using Hami melon as a green precursor (HM-CDs@ZIF-8) for analyzing TM residue in leafy vegetables through a hydrothermal method. The morphology, energy distribution of elements, particle size, crystal phase, surface functional groups, state of internal chemical bonds, and optical properties of the synthesized sensor were comprehensively characterized both before and after modification with ZIF-8. Our specific objectives were to: (1) investigate the effects of extended storage on the FL intensity and spectra of the sensor both before and after ZIF-8 modification; (2) optimize the experimental conditions to enhance the sensor's performance; (3) test the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the sensor to ensure reliable detection; (4) construct standard detection curves for TM using the sensor both before and after ZIF-8 modification. Compare the sensitivity of the sensor before and after ZIF-8 modification to assess improvements; (5) evaluate the potential of the sensor in real sample detection through spike recovery experiments. Validate the enhancement in the sensor's resistance to matrix interference before and after ZIF-8 modification; and (6) explore and elucidate the mechanism of TM detection by the sensor. By addressing these objectives, we aim to provide a thorough evaluation of the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 sensor and demonstrate its practical applicability for detecting TM residues in leafy vegetables.

Anhydrous ethanol, glucose, methanol, sucrose, phenylalanine, tartaric acid, and citric acid were produced by T-Jkemao Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). TM, phoxim, malathion, mancozeb, chlorpyrifos, indoxacarb, and primicarb were produced by Shandong Huayang Pesticide Chemical Industry Group Co., Ltd. (Taian, Shandong, China). All chlorides (nickel chloride (NiCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), barium chloride (BaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), and calcium chloride (CaCl2)) utilized for the selective and anti-interference capability testing of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, as well as sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·12H2O) and disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4·2H2O) were produced by Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Ammonia (AR), 2-methylimidazole (AR, ≥99%), and zinc acetate dihydrate (AR, ≥99%) were supplied by Beijing Psaitong Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The dialysis tube was obtained from Maige Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Taixing, Jiangsu, China). Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) and vegetables (Brassica oleracea var. capitata (kale), Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis (greens), Amaranthus cruentus (amaranth), and Cichorium endivia (Sonchus)) utilized in the study were procured from the local market (Yangling, Shaanxi, China). Potassium bromide (KBr) (SP, ≥99.9%) used in the FTIR experiments was sourced from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade methanol and ion-pairing agent were bought from Merck Drugs & Biotechnology. Quinine sulfate (98%), N, N-dimethylformamide (≥99.8%), and acetone (≥99.5%) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used directly. Ultrapure water was used in the studies.

The preparation process of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and the principle of detecting TM are shown in Fig. 1 . The hydrothermal method (Sun et al., 2023) was used. The cultivar of the selected Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) was ‘Loulanxiang’. The physical and chemical parameters of the selected Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) and its juice are shown in Supplementary Material, Table S1 and Fig. S1A. Based on the analysis of the physical and chemical parameters of Hami melon and its juice, these samples were suitable precursors for the synthesis of CDs. Hami melon had a high moisture content (90.8 ± 1.5%) and moderate hardness (209.787), which facilitated the formation of uniform CDs structures during high-temperature carbonization. Additionally, the pH value of Hami melon juice was 6.157 ± 0.098, slightly acidic, which was favorable for CDs formation. Its high soluble solid content (9.300 ± 0.300%) provided an abundant carbon source, and the negative Zeta potential (−9.273 ± 0.347 mV) indicated good dispersion, preventing aggregation during synthesis. The colorimetric parameters L* (65.403 ± 0.015), a* (16.650 ± 0.017), b* (50.427 ± 0.093), and ΔE*ab (63.480 ± 0.079) suggested good color stability of the Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) juice, which helped maintain consistent optical properties during synthesis. Therefore, Hami melon and its juice were excellent precursors for synthesizing CDs. With an average particle size of 1059 nm, Hami melon juice presented significant advantages for synthesizing CDs. The larger particle size provided a high surface area, enhancing reaction efficiency and yield. This resulted in uniform CDs with consistent sizes and improved FL properties. Additionally, the larger precursor particles facilitated surface modification and functionalization. The procedure was as follows: Initially, Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) was peeled and cut into small pieces to extract the juice. Subsequently, 10 mL of the juice and 5 mL of ammonia were added to a high-pressure reactor equipped with a Teflon liner, along with 80 mL of water. The reaction was conducted for 4 h in an oven at 180 °C. The resulting product was first filtered using qualitative filter paper, followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min. Subsequently, the filtrate was re-filtered using a 0.22 μm pes membrane. Next, the product underwent dialysis in pure water for 24 h using a dialysis tube with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 Da. Finally, the standard solution using Hami melon as the precursor (HM-CDs) was obtained through vacuum freeze-drying, weighing, and dilution procedures.

To prepare the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution, 300 mg of 2-methylimidazole was dissolved in 160 mL of methanol. Subsequently, 20 mL of HM-CDs solution was added dropwise to the mixture and stirred for 10 min. Then, 1.2 g of zinc acetate dihydrate was introduced into the precursor solution and stirred magnetically at 70 °C for 2 h. Finally, the resulting HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was obtained by washing the reaction product three times with ethanol and distilled water. The purified product was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

Upon achieving equilibrium after the addition of TM to HM-CDs@ZIF-8, the mixture's FL intensity was measured using an FL spectrophotometer. Utilizing the standard detection curve equation, the quantity of TM was determined. In the dispersed state of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, molecular rotation or vibration led to the dissipation of excited state energy through non-radiative processes, resulting in weak or absent luminescence. With the addition of TM, hydrogen bonding induced the aggregation of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM, limiting the internal movement of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 molecules. Consequently, energy release occurred through radiative processes, leading to a pronounced enhancement in luminescence- a phenomenon termed aggregation induced emission (AIE) (Zhang, Jiang, et al., 2023).

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Sigma 300, Zeiss, Germany) was utilized to examine the energy distribution and energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDX) of HM-CDs@ZIF-8. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was obtained using a JEM-2100F microscope (Japan Electronics, Japan). A nanolaser particle sizer (Zen3600, Malvern, UK) was used to study the particle size distribution of Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) juice and HM-CDs@ZIF-8 by dynamic light scattering (DLS). The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) with a scanning angle set from 5 to 60° was used to study the crystal structure of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 + TM and HM-CDs@ZIF-8. A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet iS20, Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to explore the functional groups of HM-CDs@ZIF-8. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted utilizing a K-Alpha+ instrument (Thermo Scientific, USA), where peak positions underwent recalibration via adjusting the C 1s peak to 284.8 eV. FL spectra and intensities of HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8 were evaluated using an FL spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan) with 5 nm excitation and emission slits. The ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectrum of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was measured using a UV spectrophotometer (UV-2600i, Shimadzu, Japan). The pH value of Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) juice was measured by a pH meter (100 pro+, Shanghai Lichen instrument technology, China). The chroma information of Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) juice was measured by a spectrophotometer (CS-820, Hangzhou CHNSpec Technology, China). The hardness and other physical parameters of the Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) were measured by a texture analyzer (TA.XT PLUS/50, Stable Micro Systems, UK). The soluble solid content of Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) juice was determined using a digital refractometer (PR-101a, ATAGO, Japan). The moisture content in Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) juice was measured by a forced-air drying oven (101-1AB, Tianjin Taisite Instrument, China).

2.0 mg of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was directly affixed onto the conductive adhesive. The gold coating was applied using the Quorum SC7620 sputtering coating instrument for 45 s at a rate of 10 mA. The acceleration voltage during morphology photography was set to 3 kV, while for EDX spectrum mapping photography, it was set to 15 kV. Following the acquisition of SEM and energy distribution images, energy spectrum scanning was conducted.

The HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution underwent ultrasonication for 15 min before being dropped onto a copper mesh. After the drying process, the specimen was placed into an electron microscope for morphological observation. The operational voltage utilized was 200 kV.

A 1 mL volume of the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution or Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) juice underwent ultrasonication for 15 min, after which it was transferred into a transparent colorimetric dish (DTS0012). The specimen was then introduced into the nanolaser particle size analyzer for quantification. The scanning range was set from 0.1 to 10,000 nm, with the temperature maintained at 25 °C. Water was used as the dispersant in the experiment. Each sample measurement was conducted for a duration of 60 s, and this process was repeated three times to calculate the average value.

First, the interior of the Malvern Potentiometric Sample Cell (DTS1070) was rinsed three times with ultrapure water. Then, it was wetted three times with the solution to be measured (Cucumis melo var. reticulatus (Hami melon) juice, HM-CDs@ZIF-8, TM, or HM-CDs@ZIF-8 + TM), filled to the mark with the solution to be measured, and ensured that there were no air bubbles. Finally, the cuvette was covered and placed into the sample chamber. The ambient temperature was set to 25 °C, water was selected as the solvent, and the measurement was repeated three times, each lasting 120 s, and the measurement was conducted.

At first, the freeze-dried HM-CDs@ZIF-8 or HM-CDs@ZIF-8 + TM were ground with a mortar. Following this, the obtained powder sample was meticulously positioned in the central groove of the sample holder, ensuring a slight elevation of the loose powder above the holder's surface. Subsequently, the sample holder was placed into the specified slot on the sample card to aid in the testing process. The experimental parameters were established as follows: An operating voltage of 40 kV, a current of 40 mA, and a scanning range spanning from 5 to 60°.

Initially, the KBr was dried at 110 °C for 4 h. Subsequently, the dried KBr was compacted into a thin sheet for background measurement. Following this, 2.0 mg of freeze-dried sample (HM-CDs@ZIF-8 or HM-CDs@ZIF-8 + TM) was mixed with 200 mg of KBr and ground before being pressed into a thin sheet. Lastly, the sample underwent scanning measurements in the FTIR spectrometer. The measurement process was carried out three times, and the average was calculated.

The XPS parameters were configured as follows: Alka rays were utilized as the excitation source with a photon energy of 1486.6 eV. The operating voltage was set to 12.5 kV, while the filament current remained constant at 16 mA. The photoelectron acquisition angle was established at 90°, and the X-ray incidence angle was set to 60°. The double beam underwent neutralization and testing, with the signal being accumulated over multiple cycles (n). The entire range of transmitted energy was measured at 100 eV, with a narrower range of 30 eV. The increment between each measurement was fixed at 0.1 eV. The duration of energy residence within the system ranged from 40 to 50 ms. The focal point size was determined to be 400 μm, and the device's work function was calculated as 4.2 eV. An appropriate amount of freeze-dried HM-CDs@ZIF-8 sample was applied to the sample plate, and the specimen was introduced into the designated chamber of the apparatus for testing purposes.

Initially, 1 mL of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 underwent ultrasonication for 15 min. Subsequently, the treated HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution and ultra-pure water were added to two separate colorimetric dishes. Finally, the two samples were placed into the sample chamber, the cover was closed, and the UV absorption spectrum was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long term stability and crystal structure stability
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating the long-term stability of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 is crucial to ascertain its suitability for prolonged storage after preparation. Both the HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8 were subjected to long-term stability testing, where alterations in FL intensities and spectra were measured at 2-day intervals for a duration of 30 days. When not being measured, both HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8 were kept in an environment at 4 °C.

XRD characterization of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 before and after their reaction with TM is crucial to understand the impact of the reaction on the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's crystalline structure. The experiment involved first preparing the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and recording their XRD pattern to establish the baseline crystalline structure. Subsequently, the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 were exposed to TM under controlled conditions (pH = 7.2, temperature = 25 °C, equilibrium time = 12.5 min), followed by another XRD measurement to detect any structural changes. By comparing the XRD patterns before and after the reaction, we can determine whether the interaction with TM alters the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's crystallinity, providing insights into the structural stability and potential mechanisms of interaction between HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM.

An experimental design was used to systematically investigate the effects of pH, equilibrium time and temperature on HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity. When exploring the influence of a single variable on FL intensity, other conditions were kept unchanged.

pH plays a crucial role in CDs-based sensing and detection systems (Zhang, Cheng, & Zhang, 2023), and the appropriateness of pH directly affects the results of TM content determination. Therefore, it is essential to investigate the effects of different pH conditions on the detection system. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of varied pH levels was made using 0.1 mol/L of NaH2PO4·2H2O, NaH2PO4·12H2O, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and hydrochloric acid (HCl). The HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity was measured after adding TM solution at various pH levels (pH 5.6, 6, 6.4, 6.8, 7.2, 7.6, 8, 8.4). The TM solution (0.03424 mol/L) was prepared and adjusted to the specified pH levels using HCl or NaOH. The FL intensity measurements were conducted at room temperature (25 °C) with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. After adding the TM solution, the samples were incubated for 12.5 min before measuring the FL intensity to ensure complete interaction between the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and the TM solution. The measurements at each pH were repeated three times and averaged.

When HM-CDs@ZIF-8 performs the detection of TM, the reading time of the FL intensity of the mixed solution determines whether the calculation result of the TM content is credible or not, and it is also an important index to evaluate the performance of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 to detect TM (Wu et al., 2023). Consequently, the duration required for the reaction between HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM to reach equilibrium was examined. A cuvette was used to mix 300 mL of TM solution with 1 mL of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution at room temperature (25 °C) and optimal pH (7.2). The resulting mixture's FL intensity was measured using an FL spectrophotometer at 30-s intervals for a total duration of 16 min and 30 s. The measurements were repeated three times, and the results were recorded. The average values of these results were calculated.

Temperature is an important parameter that affects the chemical reaction, and the temperature's appropriateness significantly impacts the results of the detection of TM (Hernández-Rodríguez et al., 2018). Therefore, the effect of temperature on HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was investigated at optimal pH (7.2) and equilibrium time (12.5 min). Initially, a 1 mL solution of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was aliquoted into a four-trans cuvette. Subsequently, the cuvette was immersed in water baths preheated to various temperatures (20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, and 50 °C). Upon reaching equilibrium, the cuvette was promptly removed from the water baths and subjected to FL intensity measurements using an FL spectrophotometer. This process was repeated thrice, and the average of the three measurements was calculated.

One of the critical parameters for evaluating the performance of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 is selectivity and anti-interference ability, and in the detection of real samples, interfering substances that can affect the results may be more or less present in the detection system of TM. The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed method were evaluated by reacting to common substances and ions that may be present in the detection system. The interfering substances used included glucose, sucrose, phenylalanine, tartaric acid, citric acid, phoxim, malathion, mancozeb, chlorpyrifos, indoxacarb, primicarb, nickel chloride (NiCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), barium chloride (BaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2), and calcium chloride (CaCl2), all of which were present at a concentration of 300 mg/L. The concentration of TM was 1 mg/L. In the absence of TM, 1 mL of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution was withdrawn, and 300 μL of PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.2) containing 0.09 mg of interfering substance was added. Subsequently, the mixture was oscillated for 12 min 30 s. In a cuvette, using an FL spectrophotometer, the FL intensity of the mixture was measured. In the presence of TM, 1 mL of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution was withdrawn, and 300 μL of a mixture (pH = 7.2) containing TM and interfering substance (0.09 mg) was added. The mixture was then oscillated for 12 min 30 s. Using an FL spectrophotometer, the FL intensity of the mixture was measured. The FL intensity of each sample was measured thrice, and the average was calculated. The described experimental procedure was carried out under standard room temperature and air pressure conditions, with the excitation wavelength for FL set to 375 nm.

The QY of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 in water was determined using a reference method, employing quinine sulfate as the reference substance. Quinine sulfate exhibits a QY of 55% in 0.1 M H2SO4. The calculation formula employed in this study is represented by Eq. (1).
(1)
QY =
QY s
( K /
K s
)
η /
η s
2

Where QY and QY s denote the quantum yield of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and quinine sulfate, respectively. K represents the slope of the integrated emission intensity curve with respect to absorbance for HM-CDs@ZIF-8, while K s represents the slope of the integrated emission intensity curve with respect to absorbance for quinine sulfate. η and η s represent the refractive index of the sample and the standard solvent, respectively. For the present study, η/η s was calculated to be 1.

The standard curve utilized for TM detection was established as follows: Solutions of TM at various concentrations ranging from 0.00171 to 3.4239 mol/L were prepared. Initially, 1.0 g of TM was measured using an electronic balance and then dissolved in 1000 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.2). By diluting 10 mL of this solution to a final volume of 1000 mL, a concentration of 3.4239 mol/L TM was attained. Subsequently, TM solutions spanning a concentration range of 0.0342 to 3.4239 mol/L were generated by sequentially diluting 10 mL of the aforementioned TM solution to varying volumes (12.5, 16.67, 25, 50, 100, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 mL). Additionally, TM solutions with concentrations of 0.00342 and 0.00171 were prepared by further diluting 1 mL of the 0.0342 mol/L TM solution to 10 mL and 20 mL, respectively. All these solutions were stored in brown vials.

Initially, a total of 1 mL of HM-CDs or HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution and 300 μL of TM solution were mixed together and applied to the cuvette. Then, it was vigorously agitated for 12 min and 30 s. Finally, the FL spectrophotometer's excitation wavelength was set at 375 nm to determine the mixture's FL intensity and spectrum. The mean and standard deviation were calculated after three repetitions at each TM concentration gradient. A linear fit was applied to the FL intensity and TM concentration data from the mixture to establish a standard detection curve.

To evaluate the performance of the developed sensor in real sample detection and simultaneously verify the performance enhancement of the sensor before and after modification with ZIF-8, testing was conducted on both the prepared HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8. The leafy vegetables selected were Brassica oleracea var. capitata (kale), Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis (greens), Amaranthus cruentus (amaranth), and Cichorium endivia (Sonchus). The same leafy vegetables were purchased from different stalls. Fresh samples of kale, greens, amaranth, and Sonchus were collected from local markets. The samples were transported to the laboratory in sterile plastic bags and stored at 4 °C to maintain freshness. Each sample was thoroughly washed with ultrapure water water to remove any dirt or surface contaminants and then air-dried at room temperature. The spiking amounts for each type of leafy vegetable were 2739.12, 342.39, and 34.239 μM, respectively. The specific experimental steps are as follows: 1.0 g of TM was precisely measured and dissolved in 1000 mL of PBS buffer with a pH of 7.2, and a TM solution with a concentration of 1 g/L was obtained. Then, 10 mL of the resulting solution was taken and diluted to 100 mL with PBS buffer (pH = 7.2) to obtain a TM solution with a concentration of 100 mg/L. Additionally, 1 mL of the above solution (100 mg/L) was taken and diluted to 1.25, 10, and 100 mL, respectively, to obtain TM solutions with concentrations of 80, 10, and 1 mg/L. 2.0 g of kale, greens, amaranth, and Sonchus were weighed using an electronic scale, and 1 mL of each TM solution at concentrations of 8, 1, and 0.1 mg/L was sprayed onto the surface. The treatment was conducted three times per sample at 25% relative humidity, 25 ± 2 °C, atmospheric pressure, and without wind. The samples were left in this environment for approximately 48 h to evaporate the moisture on their surfaces.

In a centrifuge tube, a dry sample and 10 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.2) were mixed and shaken for 5 min. A volume of 300 μL of the combination was introduced into a cuvette that already contained 1 mL of a standard solution of HM-CDs or HM-CDs@ZIF-8. After a duration of 12 min and 30 s, the FL spectrophotometer's excitation wavelength was adjusted to 375 nm. The FL intensity of the mixed solution was measured. Three measurements were taken in parallel for each sample. The measured FL intensity was used to calculate the detected TM content by inserting it into the standard curve. The TM content obtained from three measurements was averaged to calculate the recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) of the real samples.

We utilized HPLC (LC-2030 plus, Shimadzu, Japan) to quantify TM residue in leafy vegetables, aiming to validate the efficacy of the prepared sensor. The leafy vegetable selected was kale. Chromatographic separation was carried out using a C18 analytical column (250 mm × 4.6 mm I.D., 5 μm), with the column temperature maintained at 45 °C. Under isocratic elution conditions, the mobile phase consisted of a mixture of methanol (40%) and an ion-pairing agent (60%) flowing at a rate of 1.25 mL/min.

The SEM image of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, as depicted in Fig. 2A, revealed a polyhedral morphology (Zhang, Hu, et al., 2023) with diameters exceeding 150 nm. The surface exhibited roughness and irregularities, potentially attributed to the incorporation of HM-CDs. These surface features suggested that HM-CDs were not merely surface-bound but were potentially integrated within the ZIF-8 structure, affecting its overall morphology. This integration could have enhanced the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's functionality, as the rough surface increased the surface area, potentially improving the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's interaction with TM.

Fig. 2B illustrates the energy dispersive spectral layered image of HM-CDs@ZIF-8. Fig. 2C, D, E, and F, respectively, present the energy distributions of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and zinc (Zn) elements on HM-CDs@ZIF-8, demonstrating uniform energy distributions across the four elements, thereby confirming the successful synthesis of HM-CDs@ZIF-8. A uniform distribution indicated that the HM-CDs were evenly incorporated throughout the ZIF-8 framework. This even distribution was essential for ensuring consistent properties throughout the material, as it prevented localized areas of higher or lower concentration that could lead to performance inconsistencies or structural weaknesses. At the same time, this was essential to maintain the structural integrity and functional properties of HM-CDs@ZIF-8.

Moreover, the uniformity in elemental distribution suggested that the synthesis process was robust and reproducible, ensuring consistent performance in potential applications. The presence of C and N indicated successful doping of ZIF-8 with HM-CDs, which was likely to enhance the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's optical properties. The O presence might have indicated partial oxidation, which could influence the electronic properties of the material. Zn, being a fundamental component of ZIF-8, ensured the structural framework remained intact.

The SEM and energy distribution analyses collectively provided compelling evidence of the successful synthesis and integration of HM-CDs within the ZIF-8 framework, resulting in a composite material with potentially enhanced functional properties.

Fig. 2G displays the EDX spectrum of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, indicating their elemental composition as 76.73% C, 12.4% N, 6.15% O, and 4.72% Zn. The elevated C content was likely due to the prevalence of aromatic compounds in HM-CDs@ZIF-8. The N and O contents were likely due to the presence of abundant CN, NH, CO, and CO bonds in HM-CDs@ZIF-8. The presence of Zn could be attributed to ZnO bonds. Fig. 2H shows the TEM image of HM-CDs@ZIF-8. The HM-CDs were successfully embedded on the surface of ZIF-8, which had the shape of a rhombic dodecahedron with a diameter of about 220 nm, which was in agreement with the characterization results of the previous work (Zhang, He, et al., 2022).

The particle size distribution of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 is shown in Fig. 2I, and it can be seen that its particle size was about 220 nm, consistent with the results obtained from TEM and SEM characterization. The consistency across TEM, SEM, and DLS results reinforced the reliability of the synthesized HM-CDs@ZIF-8 particles and indicated a successful incorporation of HM-CDs into the ZIF-8 structure, which maintained a relatively narrow size distribution. This uniform and narrow particle size indicated good dispersion and stability in aqueous solutions, which prevented aggregation and was essential for consistent performance. These characteristics contributed to high sensitivity and selectivity, as well as robustness against complex substrates, in applications that detect TM residue.

The crystal structure of HMCDs@ZIF-8 was investigated through the XRD technique. Fig. 3A shows the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's XRD spectrum. The strong diffraction peak between 20 and 30° (23.5°) corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating the amorphous carbon phase of HM-CDs@ZIF-8. Peaks between 5 and 20°can be attributed to ZIF-8 patterns. It was in agreement with the previous findings (Zhang, Hu, et al., 2023). It suggested a high degree of disorder and the presence of numerous defects and functional groups on the carbon structure, which can enhance the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's adsorption capacity.

An analysis of the functional groups on the surface of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was conducted using FTIR (Fig. 3B). The presence of C-O-C bond stretching vibration peaks was revealed by the peak at 1093 cm−1 (Ma, Li, et al., 2020). The absorption band at 1433 cm−1 was determined to be the result of CN bonding (Alanazi et al., 2024). The presence of two prominent absorption peaks at around 1632 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CO and NH bonds (Zhang et al., 2021). The absorption peak at 3421 cm−1 was recognized as the stretching vibration peak of the NH bond (Jayaweera et al., 2019). Broad peaks spanning from 3260 to 3600 cm−1 signify OH stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of -OH groups (Feghhi et al., 2023). At 736 cm−1, the presence of -NH2 groups (Qi et al., 2023) on HM-CDs@ZIF-8's surface was indicated. Additionally, peaks at 1410 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 corresponded to CC bonds in the benzene ring of the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 structure. The cluster of peaks within the 1300–1500 cm−1 range indicated aromatic compound presence (Guan et al., 2024). From the aforementioned outcomes, it could be inferred that the surface of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 contains secondary amino groups. In general, the existence of the aforementioned functional groups greatly enhanced the hydrophilicity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8.

The XPS method is commonly employed to chemically analyze CDs, offering intricate details concerning the elemental composition and bonding properties of the atoms within. The chemical bond of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was determined by XPS, as shown in Fig. 3C. The spectrum indicated that the material was composed of C, O, N, and Zn. This suggested that oxygen and nitrogen groups had altered the carbon skeleton. Three distinct peaks were seen in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 3D) at energy levels of 284.80, 285.04, and 287.51 eV. These peaks indicated the presence of CC /C = C, CN, and CO in HM-CDs@ZIF-8 (Wang, Cao, et al., 2022). The N 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 3E) exhibited prominent peaks of C-N-C and NH, with binding energies of 399.52 and 400.13 eV, respectively (Narimani et al., 2022). Meanwhile, In the high-resolution XPS spectrum at 532.64, 531.93, and 531.22 eV, O 1s (Fig. 3F) detected three peaks that corresponded to HO-C=O, C-OH, and -OH groups (Falguni et al., 2023). Two signal peaks of 1021.68 eV (ZN-O) and 1044.78 eV (Zn 2P1/2) could also be found in the Zn 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 3G). The results indicated a significant presence of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of HM-CDs@ZIF-8. This contributed to the high hydrophilicity and excellent stability of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 in aqueous dispersion.

The surface of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was successfully changed with -CONH- groups, as confirmed by the examination of FTIR and XPS data. The interaction between HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM suggested the formation of an intermolecular hydrogen bond. The appearance of the AIE phenomena in HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was a result of the restriction of the non-radiative channel due to the creation of hydrogen bonds. Consequently, the FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was substantially enhanced, achieving the objective of detecting TM content.

An investigation was conducted on the optical characteristics of HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8. Fig. 3H is the FL spectra of the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 solution. The FL excitation wavelength peak was 375 nm, producing a relatively strong FL at 732 nm. It could be seen that HM-CDs@ZIF-8 had a large Stokes shift (357 nm), which indicated that HM-CDs@ZIF-8 has superior detection performance. Before being excited, HM-CDs@ZIF-8 appeared pale white; after being excited, HM-CDs@ZIF-8 appeared bright white. Fig. 3I shows the FL spectra of HM-CDs with an excitation wavelength of 386 nm and an emission wavelength of 475 nm. Before excitation (386 nm), HM-CDs appeared pale yellow, and after HM-CDs appeared green. The HM-CDs exhibited a small Stokes shift (89 nm), indicating that during TM detection, the influence of the detected background and matrix might have been significant. The QY of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was measured to be 38.32%, suggesting notably high efficiency. This high QY indicated that the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 had excellent fluorescent properties, which were crucial for its application as a sensitive sensor. The results above indicated that the developed sensor exhibited excellent performance.

The UV absorption spectrum of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 is shown in Fig. 4A, showing a very wide UV absorption band in the range of 200–600 nm, among which there were strong absorption peaks near 220 nm and 345 nm, which may be due to the existence of the aromatic π orbit HM-CDs@ZIF-8. This resulted from the π-π* transition of the CC double bond and the n-π* transition of the C=N/C=O bond (Li et al., 2017).

The variation of FL intensity over time for HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8 after preparation is shown in Fig. 4B. Within 30 days, the FL intensity of HM-CDs increased by 11.19%, while the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity continued to increase by 4.06%, which was within the acceptable range. The HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity gradually stabilized after that. The FL intensity variation of the sensor was reduced by 7.13% compared with that of HM-CDs before ZIF-8 modification. The FL intensity changes of HM-CDs were irregular, whereas those of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 showed a stronger regularity and smaller standard deviation. This can be attributed to the enhanced stability of FL after ZIF-8 modification. Fig. 4C and D depict the FL spectra changes of HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8, respectively, within 30 days after preparation. It could be observed that within this period, the FL intensity of both HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8 gradually increased over time, with no significant shift in emission wavelength for either. It showed that HM-CDs@ZIF-8 has superior performance and good long-term stability and can be stored long after preparation.

The crystal structure stability test result of the sensor is shown in Supplementary Material, Fig. S1B. Based on the XRD analysis, it can be concluded that the crystalline structure of the samples did not undergo significant changes upon the addition of TM. The key diffraction peaks observed in both HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and HM-CDs@ZIF-8 + TM samples are located at the same 2θ positions, indicating consistent lattice parameters and crystalline structure. Moreover, the shape and width of these peaks remained largely unchanged, suggesting that the crystallinity and grain size of the samples were not significantly affected. Additionally, the peak intensities were comparable between the two samples, further supporting the notion that the relative crystallinity and phase content remained stable. Therefore, it can be reasonably inferred that the formation of hydrogen bonds did not induce notable alterations in the crystalline structure of the HM-CDs@ZIF-8.
The above conclusions indicated that HM-CDs@ZIF-8 had high stability and excellent performance.

The effect of pH on the FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 is shown in Fig. 4E. With the increase of pH, the FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 first increased and then decreased and reached the maximum value when pH was 7.2. A lower or higher pH was not conducive to the detection of TM, resulting in inaccurate detection results. This result was similar to the conclusions of other researchers (Jalili et al., 2021). The best assay condition for this investigation was determined to have a pH value of 7.2.

The FL intensity after adding TM to HM-CDs@ZIF-8 changes with time, as shown in Fig. 4F. The FL intensity of the mixture gradually increased and became stable after about 12 min and 30 s (750 s). The fast detection speed of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 means that a significant FL signal change can be observed shortly after mixing it with the TM. This indicates that HM-CDs@ZIF-8 possesses efficient FL sensing performance, making it an ideal choice for rapidly detecting TM. The gradual equilibrium trend of FL intensity at HM-CDs@ZIF-8 over time was similar to previous findings (Zhu et al., 2021). Consequently, in the following investigations, a duration of 12 min and 30 s was employed as the period of equilibrium for the reaction involving HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM.

Fig. 4G shows the relationship between temperature and the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity. The results showed that the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity first increased and then decreased with the increase in temperature (Nethaji et al., 2024) and reached the highest value at 25 °C. The lower temperature reduced the activity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, resulting in a decrease in its FL intensity. Higher temperature may have led to the disruption of the surface structure of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, similarly decreasing its FL intensity. Both lower and higher temperatures were unfavorable for detecting TM, so the FL intensities and spectra were measured at 25 °C in subsequent experiments.

Considering that the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the developed HM-CDs@ZIF-8 is an important index to evaluate its performance, we measured the effect of possible co-existing substances or ions in the detection system on the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity. Fig. 4H and I illustrate the influence of potential co-existing interference on the detection results of TM. Regardless of the presence or absence of TM, when exposed to 375 nm light, even when the concentration of the interfering substances (300 mg/L) significantly exceeded the concentration of TM (1 mg/L), their influence on the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity was minimal. Only TM caused a considerable increase in the FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 at 732 nm. This demonstrated the sensor's high specificity and selectivity towards TM and confirmed the robustness of the FL sensor and the stability of the detection system against interference. It was not easily affected by other substances in the co-existing detection system during the real sample detection.

Supplementary Material, Fig. S2A demonstrates how the FL spectra of HM-CDs affect the concentration of TM (0.00171–3.4239 mol/L). As the concentration of TM increased, the peak height of the FL spectra emitted by HM-CDs gradually rose. There was a linear positive correlation between the concentration of TM (y b) and the FL intensity of HM-CDs (x b), as demonstrated in Supplementary Material, Fig. S2B. The specific linear relationship equation is provided in Eq. (2).
(2)
y b
= 1279.427
x b
+ 1236.558
The coefficient of determination R 2 = 0.9959 for Eq. (2). Eq. (2) served as the standard curve for detecting TM using HM-CDs.

Supplementary Material, Fig. S2C depicts the impact of the FL spectra of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 on the concentration of TM (0.00171–3.4239 mol/L). The results showed that the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity gradually increased with the increase of TM concentration, and the two were linearly positively correlated. The relationship between TM concentration (x) and FL intensity (y) is shown in Supplementary Material, Fig. S2D. The detection technique consistently yielded dependable results with small standard deviations for the same TM concentration. The findings demonstrated the high reproducibility of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and offered a solid foundation for future studies. The equation representing the linear relationship between the two variables is provided in Eq. (3).
(3)
y = 2103.002 x + 1252.44

The coefficient of determination of Eq. (3) was R 2 = 0.9984, indicating a high correlation between HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and the concentration of TM. Compared to the sensor before modification with ZIF-8, there was a slight increase in R 2. The Eq. (3) functioned as a detection curve for detecting TM using HM-CDs@ZIF-8. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by Eq. (4).
(4)
LOD = 3 σ / K
where σ and K are the standard deviation of the measured values of 11 blank samples and the slope of the detection curve, respectively. The LOD of HM-CDs and HM-CDs@ZIF-8 were calculated to be 29.588 and 2.025 μmol/L, respectively. After modification with ZIF, the LOD of HM-CDs was significantly enhanced. The results demonstrated the strong potential of utilizing HM-CDs@ZIF-8 for detecting TM residue. In comparison to the detection methods suggested by other researchers (as shown in Table 1 ), the FL sensor based on HM-CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited remarkable sensitivity in detecting TM. Meanwhile, the proposed detection method demonstrated a broad linear detection range, proving the sensor's excellent detection performance and the advancement of this research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 for TM detection
ce:section.ce:para: We deduced that the interaction mechanism between HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM occurred via the establishment of intermolecular hydrogen bonds. These bonds constrained non-radiative pathways within HM-CDs@ZIF-8, leading to a notable enhancement in FL intensity. Essentially, the formation of hydrogen bonds between HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM catalyzed the onset of the AIE phenomenon.

Zeta potential serves as a critical gauge for evaluating the stability of colloidal dispersion systems. The Zeta potentials of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM solutions were individually assessed. Results revealed initial Zeta potentials of 24.23 mV and − 31.57 mV for HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM, respectively (Supplementary Material, Fig. S3A). The stability of these solutions in water was found to be compromised. However, upon their amalgamation, the zeta potential of the entire system surged to 38.13 mV, indicating a propensity for particle dispersion or dissolution within the mixed solution. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the establishment of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the CO or -NH- groups in TM and the amide groups in HM-CDs@ZIF-8. The formation of hydrogen bonds constrained the non-radiative pathways of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, ultimately resulting in the emission of internal energy in the form of radiation (FL).

Through analysis of FTIR spectra and XPS data, it was determined that the -CONH- groups on the surface of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 underwent successful modification. Comparison of infrared spectra revealed a 23 cm−1 shift in the stretching frequency of the -NH- groups in HM-CDs@ZIF-8 upon the addition of TM, consistent with findings previously reported by Other researchers (Das & Ghosh, 2017; Hansen, 2021; Hansen et al., 2021). This shift indicated the formation of hydrogen bonds on the -NH- groups (Supplementary Material, Fig. S3B). To delve deeper into this phenomenon, we investigated changes in the FL emission spectra of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 in various solvents, namely acetone, N, N-dimethylformamide, water, ethanol, and methanol. Acetone and N, N-dimethylformamide are considered non-protonic solvents. Notably, in non-protonic solvents, the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity was observed to be higher in N, N-dimethylformamide compared to acetone, which solely contains CO groups. This disparity can be attributed to the presence of CO and CN groups in N, N-dimethylformamide, which serve as hydrogen bond acceptors.

In order to exclude the effect of solvent on FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, FL intensity experiments of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 in different solvents were carried out. The influence of solvents on the FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 is illustrated in Supplementary Material, Fig. S3C. Methanol, ethanol, and water are classified as protonic solvents, with their polarity following the order of water > methanol > ethanol. Surprisingly, in the mentioned solvents, there was no notable red shift observed in the FL emission peak of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, and the FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 did not exhibit an increase with the polarity of the solvent. This outcome dismisses the possibility of solvent-induced FL enhancement. In protonic solvents, the electron-repelling effect exerted by the ethyl group present in the ethanol structure surpassed that of the methyl group, leading to a higher electron cloud density around the oxygen atom compared to methanol and water. Consequently, ethanol was more inclined to accept protons than the other two substances. The findings indicated that in ethanol, the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity was notably higher than that in water and marginally higher than that in methanol. It can be inferred that during the hydrogen bond formation process, -CONH- groups tended to act as hydrogen bond donors, and the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity in different solvents exhibited a positive correlation with the proton-accepting ability of the respective solvent. Taking into account the presence of groups in the TM structure that readily accept protons and form hydrogen bonds, the HM-CDs@ZIF-8's FL intensity was enhanced upon the addition of TM due to the formation of hydrogen bonds with TM. Additionally, the experimental results related to Zeta potential corroborated the formation of hydrogen bonds between TM molecules and HM-CDs@ZIF-8.

The mechanism of FL emission is as follows: before excitation, HM-CDs@ZIF-8 are in the ‘ground state’. When exposed to an excitation light source (375 nm), the electrons in the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 transition from a low energy level to a higher energy level, entering an ‘excited state’. As the excited state decays and the electrons return to the ‘ground state’, energy is released in the form of FL (radiation), which we can observe. The schematic diagram of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 detecting TM through AIE phenomenon is shown in Supplementary Material, Fig. S4. Under the excitation of a 375 nm light source, the electrons in HM-CDs@ZIF-8 transitioned from a low energy level to a higher energy level, entering an ‘excited state’. Prior to the addition of TM, HM-CDs@ZIF-8 were dispersed in water, where their intramolecular rotation and vibration were not restricted. This led to most of the energy being released in non-radiative forms, preventing the electrons from directly returning to the ‘ground state’. Consequently, the FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was relatively weak. When HM-CDs@ZIF-8 were mixed with TM, the CO or -NH- groups in TM formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds with the amide groups in HM-CDs@ZIF-8. The formation of these hydrogen bonds restricted the intramolecular rotation and vibration of HM-CDs@ZIF-8, causing HM-CDs@ZIF-8 and TM to bind closely together, forming a localized aggregation state. This restriction reduced the energy released in non-radiative forms, allowing the electrons in HM-CDs@ZIF-8 to return to the ‘ground state’ from the ‘excited state’. Consequently, FL (radiation) was emitted, triggering the AIE phenomenon, which significantly enhanced the FL intensity.

Therefore, it can be concluded that the secondary amides modified on the surface of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds with the CO and CN groups in the TM structure, thereby triggering the occurrence of the AIE phenomenon.

In order to explore the practicability of the developed sensor in the detection of TM in real samples and simultaneously verify the performance enhancement of the sensor before and after modification with ZIF-8, the potential of the proposed TM residue detection method was evaluated through spiking experiments using HM-CDs or HM-CDs@ZIF-8 as the sensor. The FL intensity of the mixture solution was substituted into the standard curve, and the real sample's recovery rate and RSD were calculated (Table 2 ). Prior to ZIF-8 modification, the sensor (HM-CDs) exhibited spiking recovery rates ranging from 101% to 118%, accompanied by RSDs spanning 0.11% to 3.41%. Following ZIF-8 modification (HM-CDs@ZIF-8), spiking recovery rates fell within the range of 96% to 105%, with RSDs ranging from 0.26% to 2.55%. This suggested that the sensor's capability to counteract interference from coexisting substances in the matrix was moderately improved after ZIF-8 modification. The proposed detection method was not easily disturbed by the matrix but also had the advantages of low cost, fast detection speed, convenience, simplicity, and strong anti-interference ability.

As outlined in Supplementary Material, Table S2, the analytical results obtained through HPLC were consistent (P > 0.05) with those obtained using HM-CDs@ZIF-8. This agreement indicates that HM-CDs@ZIF-8 provides a highly reliable and practical method for detecting TM in real samples.

Based on the hydrothermal method, we prepared a fluorescent probe based on ZIF-8-modified carbon dots with super large Stokes shift (357 nm) using Hami melon as a green precursor for analyzing TM residue in leafy vegetables. The morphology of the synthesized sensor, energy distribution of elements, particle size, crystal phase, surface functional groups, state of internal chemical bonds, and optical properties before and after modification with ZIF-8 were comprehensively characterized. HM-CDs@ZIF-8 had the shape of a rhombohedral dodecahedron with a particle size of about 220 nm and an amorphous structure. The excitation wavelength was 375 nm, and the emission wavelength was 732 nm. The QY of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 was measured to be 38.32%. Under the optimal detection conditions (pH = 7.2, temperature = 25 °C, atmospheric pressure, balance time = 12.5 min), there was a good linear relationship between the concentration (0.00171–3.4239 mol/L) of TM and the FL intensity of HM-CDs@ZIF-8. R 2 = 0.9984, and LOD was 2.025 μmol/L. Compared to the sensor before modification with ZIF-8, the LOD (29.588 μmol/L) was significantly improved, and the R 2 (0.9959) was slightly increased. In the selectivity experiment, the sensor showed excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability to 21 substances that may coexist in the detection system. In the long-term stability test (30 days), it was found that the fluorescence intensity of the sensor was increased by 11.19% before being modified by ZIF-8, and the long-term stability of the sensor was increased by 7.13% compared with 4.06% after being modified by ZIF-8. Moreover, the crystal structure of the sensor did not change significantly after reacting with TM, indicating that it had good stability. The mechanism of using HM-CDs@ZIF-8 to detect TM can be explained as follows: The presence of secondary amino groups in HM-CDs@ZIF-8 allows for hydrogen bonding with TM upon addition, leading to the occurrence of AIE phenomenon, thereby enhancing FL and achieving the purpose of TM detection. In the experiment of four kinds of real vegetable samples, before modification with ZIF-8, the sensor's spiking recovery rates ranged from 101% to 118%, with relative standard deviations between 0.11% and 3.41%. After modification with ZIF-8, the spiking recovery rates ranged from 96% to 105%, with RSDs between 0.26% and 2.55%. This indicated that the sensor's ability to resist interference from coexisting substances in the matrix was enhanced to some extent due to the modification with ZIF-8. The sensor was simple, efficient, rapid, sensitive, low cost, stable, and reliable, and it had a good potential for application in detecting TM residue in leafy vegetables.

In conclusion, this study contributed several novel contributions to the field of fluorescent probes and pesticide residue detection. Firstly, we developed a green synthesis method for fluorescent CDs using Hami melon as a precursor, offering an environmentally friendly alternative to traditional methods. Secondly, we innovatively combined these HM-CDs with ZIF-8 to create HM-CDs@ZIF-8 composites, which leverage the exceptional chemical stability and high surface area of ZIF-8 to enhance detection performance. Thirdly, our detailed investigation into the detection mechanism of TM by HM-CDs@ZIF-8 reveals new insights into the specific reaction pathways and FL changes, providing a theoretical foundation for future research. Meanwhile, it can provide ideas for other researchers to synthesize fluorescent probes with excellent properties. Additionally, we demonstrated the practical application of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 in detecting TM residue in leafy vegetables, highlighting the composite's potential for real-world food safety testing. Finally, the HM-CDs@ZIF-8 composite exhibited outstanding detection performance with high sensitivity and a low LOD for TM, surpassing existing detection methods. These innovations collectively can advance the field by introducing a new green synthesis route, enhancing detection capabilities, and expanding practical applications.

The synthesized HM-CDs@ZIF-8 offers several advantages for detecting TM residue, including fast detection speed, improved sensitivity, and better resistance to matrix interference. However, potential disadvantages such as stability under different environmental conditions and the cost of ZIF-8 synthesis should also be considered. Meanwhile, HM-CDs@ZIF-8 is disposable and cannot be recycled and reused, which is bound to cause waste of materials. Therefore, in the follow-up study, the reuse method of HM-CDs@ZIF-8 will be explored to save material resources and make contributions to sustainable development.

Chunhao Cao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenchuan Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
